All right, welcome back to lesson number two of straight line prospecting. We're cooking with gas here. Here's the deal. As I said at the end of the last lesson, no matter how targeted your marketing campaign is, it's not going to only bring in the right type of buyers. Hence, you'll have those four categories I mentioned at the end of the last lesson. Let me tell you exactly what they are. In any marketing campaign, the first group that you're going to find is your best buyers of all. They're called buyers in heat. Now a buyer in heat is someone that needs your product, they want your product, they can use your product, they can afford your product, and also they're feeling some type of pain or lack in their life that makes them feel out of control or just antsy, which means they want to buy it now. There's urgency involved. In other words, these are the people that have already passed the point of shopping around and they're saying, you know, I got to make a decision because something is just not right, whether it's my car is clinking and clanking and breaking down so I got to get myself a new car, you know, maybe it's some sort of plastic surgery procedure, right? Seriously. Or a toothache even. I got to go to the dentist now and get that done. It doesn't matter what you're selling. Seriously. When someone is in pain, whether like just I got to get it done now, that's your buyer in heat. And obviously that is your best buyer because there's urgency created and they're ready to buy now and they can afford it. The only problem with buyers in heat is that if we take a group, let's say of 100 buyers, they're going to be your smallest group. They might have 15 at most 20% no matter how targeted your campaign is. That's all of them that will be your buyers in heat. And those are getting your best buyers of all. The next group of buyers are called buyers in power. Now like buyers in heat, they need your product. They want your product. They can use your product and they can afford your product. The only difference is that while there might be some pain involved, they're feeling pain, it's not severe enough. It hasn't gotten to the point where they say, I got to buy right now. They still feel like they're in a position of power. Hence the name buyers in power. So they're still shopping around. They have every intention of buying, but they get away until they find the absolute best fit, the best price, the best course benefit ratio to them, the value proposition. So they have that sense of power. But again, the only thing that separates a buyer in power from a buyer in heat is that sense of almost desperate urgency that a buyer in heat has. They're in heat. They got to do it right now. But those two are your absolute best buyers in your sales funnel. And you'll have about, let's say there's 15, 20% of your buyers in heat. You'll have about, let me, 25 to 35% will be your buyers in power. These two groups are your absolute best buyers and the ones that I'm going to want you to focus on. But let's move forward now to number three. These are what I call the dreaded looky-loos or the tire kickers. Now these are the most toxic, most dangerous people who enter your sales funnel because what they do is the looky-loos or tire kickers, they disguise themselves as buyers in power. Meaning they pretend like they need your product, they want your product and they might very well need your product and want it. But the one thing is they can't afford it. They have absolutely no intention of buying it. They're window shopping. They're getting a vicarious thrill just to say, oh, let me take it for a test drive. Oh, let me try on some clothes or whatever it might be. And here's the problem. Because they disguise themselves as buyers in power, an untrained salesperson will spend a lot of time taking those people all the way down the straight line, making a full-blown sales presentation, going all the way to the clothes. And when they start to ask for the order to that phase of the sale, they start getting hit with all the objections, which in this case, they're not really stalls for uncertainty. They're just revealing the bullshit of the situation. These people have no intention of buying. And here's what happens. The disaster happens on two fronts. Number one, you waste massive amounts of time. Because remember, you have to make a full-blown straight line presentation to each one of these people who has no intention of buying. Just think of that. That's how you waste the time. Who wants to do that? I would much rather identify those people up front, say thank you, but you're not right this or whatever, send them where they can get help. Or maybe they'll be helpless if they're past the point. But you don't want to bring them down the line and make a full-blown presentation. That's why it wastes time. That's a big problem. But even worse is the second disaster, which is because you're unaware that they're lucky loser, Ty, because you start saying, wow, what's wrong with me? Is there something wrong with my closing ability? Can I not really close them? Is it my emotional certainty I'm not creating? Maybe I'm not being logical enough? Do I not ask for the order? Did I break for a poor? No. You didn't do anything wrong. In fact, you did everything right but one thing. You made presentations to these people in the first place. So what happens is for untrained salespeople, you get this bad read. You say, God, maybe sales is not for me. I don't close at a high enough level and it's taking me all day and no one's saying yes because you're talking to people who aren't real buyers. You get it? So that negativity, the limiting beliefs that form as a result of that are so toxic and debilitating that unless you learn how to get these people out of the funnel, they sabotage your success and actually could almost say, you know what, sales ain't for me when actually at every town within here and up here and deserved the wealth success that comes from being a top producer but these dreaded lucky losers talked you out of it because you didn't know how to get rid of them. So what does that mean? Well what it means is that we have to learn how to identify them quickly and elegantly and then remove them out of the pipeline in a way that's not offensive. You don't want to be mean to them, right? But you just get rid of them as soon as you can and then only spend your time talking to those first two groups. So what percentage, first of all, are lucky losers? It depends again on how targeted your marketing is and what your price point is but they could account for 30, 35% of the people who disguise themselves as buyers in heat. So let me tell you the telltale signs. Number one, your typical looky loob almost has too much information about your product. They already know it all. They're almost answering their own questions or they're like leading you because why? Because they're shopping around again and again and again talking to every sales person to get that vicarious feel like they almost are buying and it makes them some dope bean rush, whatever it might be, right? And they know way too much about the product. That's number one. Number two, they often overly emote. So when you say they'll say oohs and ahs and they just get this weird energy like you're saying this person is a bit too excited almost like it's not real like they're almost over because they're acting, right? They're overplaying their hand. That's the second thing that you'll know and when you see that just raise the awareness, right? That's the second point. Number three, when you start to get to the actual questions that you ask and we're going to go through those in the next lessons, right? So what questions do you ask? Some of those questions are going to be based on finance. You're trying to find out if someone directly is financially qualified. When you do that with a looky loo, either they'll get very vague and evasive or they'll be overly boorish. Oh yeah, I got tons of money. You get it? Then don't act normally. So here's the thing. This is again, this is as much of an art as a science identifying the looky loos and I promised this you'll get really good at it quickly because when you see how toxic they are, you'll go, oh my God, I got to keep my acute, my sensory acute. I got to look at these people and really identify them. So whilst mostly art here, just remember if a few of those things come shining through those things I just spoke about, right? Or on the side of getting them out of your pipeline as quickly as you can so you don't fall into that trap of making presentations to people who will never buy from you and then sitting back and scratching your head and saying, what's wrong with me? How come I can't close? And then you quit the job and only because you were speaking to the wrong people. Make sense? I know it does. Let's go on to the last group. Now these are funny group people. They're called the mistakes. The mistakes are those people who kind of get dragged into your pipeline. They walk in the wrong store. They click on the wrong website. They opt in by mistake. They don't really want to be there. If you're doing public space like in a seminar, right? The partner goes, come and drag them into the room. You got to see this like roll in the rise the whole time. Then you try to sell them something and they'll never buy. They don't want to be there. So they're pretty easy for you to identify. So when you find those people, you start asking questions. You can quickly remove them from the pipeline and that's that. So the key is now, just remember, think be smart here. You have these four groups of buyers. So ask yourself, which two groups of buyers do you want to move down the straight line? Ask yourself, is it the buyers in heat, the buyers in power, or is it the looky lose and the mistakes? Which ones do you want to move down and make presentations to? Answer that right now. No, no, no. You don't want to bring the looky lose and the mistakes down the straight line. Listen, here's the deal. Remember, looky lose are time vampires. They waste your time pretending to be buyers in power, but they're not going to buy. They're just not. So all that time that you spend making full blown sales presentations to them, guess what? Time is money. You could have spent that time qualifying more people, find the real buyers, which are the buyers in power and buyers in heat, right? And moving them down the straight line is all identifying their needs. Now the mistakes, of course, are easier to find than the looky lose, but again, you don't want those going down the straight line either. Again, it's all about time management. Time is money. All that time spent making presentations to people who are never going to buy from you, huge error. So the correct answer is you want to bring the buyers in power and the buyers in heat down the straight line. And one more thing, in terms of those buyers in power, when you ask those pain identifying questions, you want to ask follow up questions as well. So you can actually start amplifying that pain and then turn those buyers in power into buyers in heat. So now when they listen to your presentation, they're listening for more open perspective. There's some urgency involved because they actually feel pain. That denial has been a woken up. Obviously, right? Of course, you want to make presentations to the buyers in power and the buyers in heat. And here's something really interesting to get to at the higher levels of the straight line is that what you're really doing as you go through the intelligence gathering process, you're asking these people questions, these good potential buyers, right? And by finding out where their pain lies, you can actually start amplifying their pain right then and there. You don't want to resolve their pain. You want to amplify that pain. And what ends up happening is you elevate the buyers in power and you turn them into buyers in heat, which makes them much more open to buying right now. So then when you go into the next portion, when you make your transition into the main body of your presentation, they're listening very closely. They're hanging on your every word because now they're insinuating to that uncomfortability of having some sort of lack in your life or being out of control. And because of that, they're much more open and more likely to want to fill that need now, especially when you've come off as the expert in your field, the trusted advisor, not the grand inquisitor, a trusted advisor, the family friend, the doctor who's going to solve their problem by filling that need quickly, elegantly, and in a way that empowers them and bam, that's what straight line prospecting is all about. And with that, it is quiz time. So please take your quiz. It's important stuff here. Go through your exercises and I'll see you in the next lesson.
